paragraphs: One of the major recreational hotspots in Karachi, and a favorite hangout for couples. It contains a large “Safari” area, which contains animals like reindeer, camels, ostriches, llamas, and various species of mountain goats, that can be viewed from a distance in their artificially-made natural environments. It also contains large open spaces for walks, and children’s attractions and swings. The highlight of the Safari Park is the large hill located in the very center. There are various options to get to the top. You could follow a meandering trail that leads to the top. Or you could walk up the steps that have been built at various spots along the hillside to climb to the top. Or, you can just prefer to “go natural” and climb the rugged hillside by yourself. At the top of the hill are lush green areas for walking, running around, and even playing football (yes, if you happen to bring one along). There are also food and ice cream stalls – but no restrooms.

Another major attraction in the Safari Park is the chair lift, which gives you a bird’s-eye view of the Park as well as the surrounding areas of the city. The tickets are definitely meager for such an entertaining experience. A pleasing addition to the Park is the “Safari Coach” service, which, at a very small fare takes you deep into the animal areas to let you view the wildlife at a closer range. An attraction for children is the mini train, which gives you a tour of the entire park.
place:safari-park.location: Main University Road, opposite Samama Shopping Center.

paragraphs: This is a one-of-a-kind museum in Pakistan. It is a kind of virtual observatory which regularly puts on shows about the solar system and cosmic phenomenon, with a large dome as the screen on which the slide shows/movies are presented. The hall inside is pretty comfortable, and the seats are just like real aeroplane seats. The shows are interesting and captivating for a first-time viewer, but, unfortunately, they have not changed much over the past few years. The shows in English are much more detailed, and are held every Sunday evening. On other days there are only Urdu shows. An interesting feature is a real PIA airplane that stands in their lawn. A ticketed, guided tour by an officer enables one to view the plane from the inside; its cockpit, pilot seats, and various other areas. Tickets for the planetarium show are Rs 45, and for the show plus the plane tour, Rs 60.
place:pia-planetarium.location: Main Hassan Square, adjacent to Expo Centre, Gulshan.

paragraphs: The most prominent of all museums in Pakistan. It contains a splendid collection of ancient objects, transcripts, and other relics from prehistoric ages to the modern history of Pakistan. The galleries are well organized and the ambience is one of care rather than neglect. A special mention are the exhibits on the Indus Valley settlements, namely the ancient Harappa, Gandhara, and the Mohen-jo-daro civilizations. Sculptures, coins, items and objects of daily use, toys, clothes, crockery and pottery, all portray the rich cultural heritage of Pakistan well before it came into being. A special section on Islam in the Subcontinent includes beautiful Islamic calligraphy samples, ancient religious manuscripts, miniature models, paintings, and the clothes and weapons of early Muslim invaders. The museum also contains an ethnological gallery which highlights the different races and religions, and the evolution and amalgamation of cultures in the Subcontinent.
place:national-museum-of-pakistan.location: Dr Ziauddin Ahmed Road, near Burns Road, Saddar.

paragraphs: This palace was built by a Hindu businessman by the name of Shivratan Chandraratan Mohatta in the 1920’s. The building is constructed out of special pink stones from Jodhpur and the common yellow stones. It housed the offices of the Foreign Affairs ministry after the founding of Pakistan. It was soon given under the care of Fatima Jinnah, sister of the Quaid, but after her death it fell into neglect and disrepair. It was not until the late 1980’s that the Sindh governor took action to restore it to its former glory. The building was renovated by skilled craftsmen (and is still under constant improvement to this day), and was converted to a splendid cultural museum. It has on display artifacts, art pieces, paintings, Islamic art samples like blue ceramics, and other cultural relics. Exhibitions are also frequently held, the most notable one being the ‘Jewel in the Crown’ exhibition which portrayed Karachi as it was under the Colonial rule, and was on display for more than a year.
place:mohatta-palace-museum.location: 7, Hatim Alvi Road, near British Consulate, Clifton.

paragraphs: This is perhaps one of the strangest landmarks in Karachi. No one is quite sure why it was built. Some say it has paranormal powers to guide alien spacecraft. Others believe that it contains within it some ancient secrets about the city. But the most commonly circulating rumor is that it was built to honour the freemasons, which is apparent from the strange encryptions and patterns on its walls. Stand around it and ask any local, and you’ll get to hear a dozen different stories as to the purpose and history of its construction. What is known for sure is that it is named after Sir William Merewether, who was a British commissioner in Sindh from 1868 to 1877 and that the tower was constructed during the years 1884-1892. The large clock in the tower was donated by the Karachi Stock Exchange.

There is no entry fee, because there is no entry – you can just view it from a few meters away, as it was enclosed by a strong fence after miscreants tried damaging what they believed to be “Zionist” symbols on its walls. You can photograph it as much as you like.

This is the final resting place of the Father of the Nation, Muhammad Ali Jinnah. Its construction started soon after his death in 1948, and it was completed in the 1960’s. Made of white marble, this is undoubtedly the most well-known landmark of Karachi, being featured in pictures and media whenever Karachi is mentioned. The mausoleum building is surrounded by a large park which was until recently, poorly maintained. Lately, there have been impressive improvements to the park, with the addition of trees, paved paths, properly built steps, eating areas, and fountains that actually work. It offers a stark contrast to the noisy and hectic atmosphere of the city just a few hundred metres away. With a quiet and peaceful ambience, the garden itself is lucrative enough to make you stay and stroll for endless hours. Climbing the many series of steps leading to the mausoleum building can take quite a while. But once on the top platform, you can, on a clear day, see a large area of the city over the horizon. You are required to take off your shoes before you enter the mausoleum’s interior. Once inside, you will be simply awed by the sheer size of the room. In the center is the grave of the Quaid, surrounded by a metal fence to keep people at a distance. Hanging from the roof is an elegant crystal chandelier which was presented as a gift by China. The mausoleum hall is guarded at all times by alert security personnel. The ceremonial changing-of-guards at the Mazar is a display of the patriotic fervor of the armed forces, and is something which should not be missed if you visit the mausoleum.

The graves of the Quaid’s sister, Fatima Jinnah, and the first Prime Minister of Pakistan, Liaqat Ali Khan, are also present nearby. There is a special museum below the mausoleum building that displays objects which were used by the Quaid. Items on display include the Quaid’s clothes, caps, coats, walking sticks, cigar, monocles, coins, and pens. Specially designed rooms contain the actual furniture that was used by the Quaid, as in his living, dining, drawing and bedrooms. There is also a splendid collection of old cars that were used by the Quaid.

The entry to the Mazar and the park was free earlier, but now a small fee is charged (Rs 5), as maintenance charges. Unlike many national monuments in foreign countries, photography and videomaking is allowed inside the park as well as the mausoleum premises, except for a few days when it may be prohibited due to security reasons, such as when a minister or foreign delegate is visiting the Mazar.

The timings of this jet fountain are from sunset to midnight, Wednesdays through Sundays. It is simply a divine sight at sunset, and can be seen from miles afar – you can even see it from the Seaview coastline, which is a long way down the coast – but the real experience is at close range. Having 18 floodlights to illuminate it against the night sky, and throwing water upwards to a height of 500-600 feet, this jet fountain is believed to be the highest of its kind in the world, and is no less than a wonder in a city like Karachi.
place:kpt-jet-fountain-view.location: Clifton coastline (get to the coast from any direction after sunset, look on the horizon, and follow the fountain to get to it!).

paragraphs: The Karachi Port Trust Building is one of the most recognizable heritage landmarks near Karachi's old port district, standing on Eduljee Dinshaw Road as the historic headquarters of the authority that manages the Port of Karachi. Built in 1915-16 and designed by architect George Wittet, the building reflects the confidence of Karachi's early twentieth-century port era, when the city was growing into one of the subcontinent's major maritime gateways.

Its broad arcaded ground floor, repetitive upper-storey window bays, dome, and classical detailing make it a strong stop for travelers interested in colonial-era Karachi, civic architecture, and the story of how the harbor shaped the city. The building is usually appreciated from the outside as part of an old-city heritage route, especially when combined with nearby landmarks around Merewether Tower, Custom House, Kharadar, and the waterfront.

A lesser-known chapter in its history is that during the First World War the offices were converted into a large hospital, adding another layer to its public role beyond port administration. Today, it remains an active institutional building, so visitors should treat it as a working government/port office rather than a museum, but it is still worth seeing for its scale, facade, and place in Karachi's maritime history.
place:karachi-port-trust-building.location: Eduljee Dinshaw Road, near Tower and the old port district.

paragraphs: Founded in 1893 and known originally as Gandhi Garden, this zoo also contains a few botanical gardens. A pretty basic amenity, but a popular hangout nevertheless. The collection of exotic birds from all over Pakistan, and also from abroad, is sure to delight. A lake serves as a natural habitat for many species of ducks, geese, and swans, and a separate section of the same lake houses alligators and crocodiles. A Reptile House (which has a separate ticket) contains exhibits with around a dozen different reptile species, including lizards, snakes, tortoises, and small crocodiles. A Natural History Museum (also with a separate ticket) houses several preserved animals, some of them believed to be extinct in Pakistan due to excessive hunting and poaching. The Mughal Garden is one of the main attractions of the zoo. It contains beautiful flower bushes and fountains. A must-visit if you want to create photographic memories. Do not worry if you have not brought along a camera. There are several professional photographers located inside the zoo who will take and develop your photographs in very little time, and at a very little cost – just make sure they do not find out you are a foreigner, in which case they could heavily overcharge you. The zoo offers horse and elephant rides for children as well as grown ups. It also has a playland/amusement park for children, with rides and games.

This park sits in the center of the residential area of PECHS. A very large public park, with a big natural hill offering a magnificent view of the city. The park is filled with lush greenery and has a huge central lake with lots of geese and colorful ducks that you can feed and talk to. Also included is a small amusement park for kids, with swings and rides, and lots of pigeons. Hill Park also has several snack joints, and a bigger restaurant, the Oasis. An ideal recreational spot for families, and a yet more ideal hangout for shy lovebirds.

This majestic Hall, constructed in a Gothic/Venetian style, was built in 1865 in honour of Sir Bartley Frere, Commissioner and later Viceroy of the region. During the Colonial days it used to serve as a center for the cultural elite of Karachi, holding regular socio-cultural activities.

Today, Frere Hall contains a large public library, the Liaquat National Library, which is one of the few good libraries in Karachi. It has more than seventy thousand books in store, including rare manuscripts, technical reports and journals, and a marvellous collection of atlases. The upper floor of the Hall is dedicated to Sadequain, a world renowned Pakistani painter and calligrapher. It is where Sadequain started painting the ceiling in brilliant colours and patterns, but he did not get a chance to complete his work as he passed away. The floor now contains some of his best masterpieces, inlcuding beautiful paintings and calligraphy samples.

The park around the Hall is called Jinnah Park, or Bagh-e-Jinnah. It is usually open to the public and its lush green lawns and leafy trees provide a soothing, comfortable escape from the frenetic life of Karachi.

Due to rising threats to security, the area near Frere Hall had been sealed off to visitors but with the shifting of the US Consulate to a new location near Mai Kolachi, Frere Hall has once again been opened to the public. Its Sunday book bazaar is a great place for bargain hunters.
place:frere-hall.location: Abdullah Haroon Road, directly opposite the Marriot Hotel.

paragraphs: Another majestic building from the British era. This market was constructed on a demolished execution site. It is another fine example of British/Gothic architecture. It was built in 1890, to mark the silver jubilee of the reign of Queen Victoria. The building itself contains four identical galleries, a large central atrium, and a 140-ft high elegant clock tower (whose clock no longer functions). As a marketplace, Empress Market attracts large crowds of customers from all around the city, as everything of general everyday use, especially foodstuff, is available at quite low prices. You can see how the locals interact, haggle and buy. But the market proves just as satisfying for those interested in ancient architecture and monuments. No tickets. Feel free to roam about the vicinity, in and out, as many times as you wish.

Constructed along 3.7 kilometers of coast, and spread over 80 acres of land, this park is reputed to be the largest park in Asia. Needless to say, it adds considerably to the beauty of the beach. Its attractions include unique rose bushes, lush green lawns, tall trees, beautifully crafted fountains, well placed wooden and stone benches, well maintained restrooms, clean paved pathways, children’s play areas, large dinosaur statues, a turtle pond with live turtles, and food courts and souvenir shops.

The beach, commonly called Clifton, is a long stretch of shore. It extends from southern parts of Karachi to the northwestern Karachi coasts, where it merges with the eastern coasts of Baluchistan. The common areas of the beach ideal for recreation include: The Clifton area, also called Seaview; The Sandspit area (a long drive from the main Clifton beach, much quieter and calmer, ideal picnic spot if you can rent a hut there); Hawkesbay area (clean sandy shores and clear and cool waters; also is a natural breeding ground for the endangered Green Turtle); Paradise Point (famous for high tides and dangerous rocks which have been severely eroded by water and wind to form natural caves and arches); Gadani (just outside Karachi, inside Baluchistan; famous for its rocks and clear waters).

If you happen to visit Karachi during the peak summer months, be sure you do not miss out on the beach. The Clifton/Seaview area is the most popular hangout in the summers, and it is very crowded. Food stalls and green tea vendors can be seen everywhere. Horse and camel rides are ready to be taken, at just around Rs 30-50. If you want to avoid crowds, it is better to head for the Sandspit or the Hawkesbay area, which are not very far. And if you want to dive into total solitary serenity, make your way to Gadani or Paradise Point. But make sure you travel with reliable people, as these areas can be dangerous, especially for foreigners.

One of the largest and most popular spots in Karachi, consisting of an amusement park, an adjoining water park, and a shopping mall. The amusement park has plenty of rides and other attractions for families, and ticket prices are quite reasonable. Entrance is only granted to families/couples.

The water park has a water slide which is reputed to be the largest in Asia. The Aladdin shopping area is open to all, and has a large variety of items ranging from garments to horrific masks. Prices may be a bit expensive due to the fact that families visiting the amusement parks often visit the mall to shop.
place:aladin-park-legacy-site.location: Main Rashid Minhas Road, near Millenium Mall, Gulshan.

paragraphs: This building has large exhibition halls that host interesting activities almost all year round. Prime highlights include the Lifestyle exhibition, large book fairs, various displays and exhibitions of large multinational companies, trade promotion fairs, local products exhibitions, bridal exhibitions, science and technology displays, and educational fairs. You are sure to catch an event at the Expo Centre any time you visit Karachi.

Yep, you heard that right. Sattar Buksh is Pakistan’s very own take on Starbucks, the very popular American coffeehouse chain, and that’s the reason why it’s one of the hottest spots right now in Karachi.

Despite having a similar logo and concept, Sattar Buksh brings a bit of Karachi into your visit by adding things like ‘karak chai’ (a popular milky sweet tea) and fried eggs with ‘paratha’ (a type of bread) on to the menu, just so that you’re not entirely forgetting which country you’re visiting while enjoying that American coffee and international cuisine.
So get ready to put on one of their signature mustaches and sip one of the best coffees in town, as this place is one you definitely do not want to miss out on!
Timings: 12pm – 12am daily
restaurant:sattar-buksh.location: F73/1 Clifton Block 4, opposite the Russian Consulate.

paragraphs: One of the more picturesque areas of Karachi, Port Grand is home to a unique shopping and dining experience while running parallel to Native Jetty Bridge that connects the Karachi Port Trust to Keamari and is quite the historical location. There is really no better place to appreciate the Karachi sunset, along with the occasional boating fishermen, some ships and containers.
Port Grand welcomes families only, has an entrance fee of Rs. 600/- and timings from 6pm to 11pm daily.

The best Nihari around Karachi, at unbelievably low prices! The varieties include Plain Nihari, Maghaz (Brain) Nihari, Paya Nihari, and Chicken Nihari. Each dish is equally mouthwatering. The restaurant is very crowded, and it is the only eating place in Karachi that has to close early at night as all the Nihari always finishes by 10 pm at the most. If you want to have a good dining experience, reach by 8 pm or so, and you’ll be lucky to wait for only a small time for a table to be vacated. A large family hall is located upstairs.

Burns Road, Saddar, was declared the first official food street in Karachi pretty recently, but has, from the very beginning, always been the ideal eating place for all people from all classes of society. Located right in the heart of the city center, Burns Road is a crowded two way road where you are likely to get caught in traffic because of the double parkings on either side. The restaurants along the road are packed with people at peak hours, and the roads are packed with even more cars, making it all the more difficult for anyone else to get across the road.

That said, the number of restaurants and the variety of food served is amazing, and will spoil you for choice. Ranging from small one-room ice cream parlours to large, multi-storey diners, Burns Road offers the very best in Pakistani cuisine. Primary menus consist of the very popular Biryani, Haleem, chicken and mutton Qourma, Nihari, Paaye, and of course, Daal Chaawal. Sweet dishes include Kheer, Zarda, Rus Malai, and a wide assortment of Mithaai (confectionary).
Some of the most popular names along Burns Road include:
1. Food Center (Local) Specializes in Biryani, has separate family hall upstairs.
2. Mazedaar Haleem (Local) Specializes in Haleem, all kinds of Haleem, including ranging from the very plain to the very spicy, from chicken to mutton to kabab/kofta haleem.
3. Karachi Haleem (Local) The oringinal Haleem experts.
4. New Delhi Gola Kabab House (BBQ/Local) All the barbecue you can think of, at quite cheap prices.
5. Agha Sajji House (Local) The best Sajji (full steamed chicken) around Karachi.
6. Mela (Local) A branch of the popular food outlet chain, Mela. Has a large variety of Pakisani dishes, may seem relatively less cheap.

Founded in 1969 as a simple roadside stand, Student’s Biryani has evolved into a chain of grand restaurants, its Biryani being one of the most popular ones in Karachi. It also provides free delivery services within a limited radius.
restaurant:student-biryani.location: Behind Rainbow Center, Saddar; Burns Road, Saddar; Hasan Square, Gulshan

paragraphs: Undoubtedly one of the best local restaurants in town. Specializes in biryani. Pretty inexpensive, single-plate biryani is Rs 40, double-plate at Rs 75. Other highlights include a 20-dish buffet dinner, barbecue, fish menus, and scrumptious desserts like kheer (cold milk and rice dish) and zarda (hot, sweet rice dish).
restaurant:food-centre.location: Burns Road Food Street, Saddar; Husainabad Food Street, F.B.Area

paragraphs: A pleasant family dining experience in a relaxed environment.
restaurant:usmania-restaurant.location: University Road, Gulshan-e-Iqbal, Opposite PIA Planetarium.

paragraphs: One of the very earliest restaurants in Karachi, it has evolved only in shape and retains the high quality of its barbecue. The barbecue menu consists of chicken and mutton, both with specialties including chicken tikka, chicken boti, and chicken sangdana/kaleji (liver). The Bihari boti and mutton kabab are also worth trying.
restaurant:bundu-khan.location: Main M.A.Jinnah Road, near Numaish Sindhi Muslim Roundabout

paragraphs: Barbecue. Anything and everything barbecued. Chicken, lamb, Lebanese kababs, Afghani, Gola kababs, Reshmi kababs, Bihari kababs, Seekh kababs. Undoubtedly the best barbecue spot in Karachi. Spans over 4 floors: 3 covered, and one airy rooftop. Advisable to make a reservation in advance.
restaurant:bbq-tonight.location: Comm.5/1, Boating Basin, Block 5, Clifton. Timings: 1900 – 0100

paragraphs: One of the few authentic Chinese outlets in Karachi. A bit expensive, but good value for money. Dishes include sweet and sour treats like soups, salads, sauces, dry beef dishes, spicy chicken, fried rice, and fish and prawns. Sure treat for genuine Chinese food lovers.

This is one of the few French outlets you will find around Karachi. It is run by a genuine French national, so you can be sure to find authentic French cuisine here. It is located inside the Alliance Française, and offers the best in Parisian dining in Karachi. Café Flo is quite popular with foreigners residing permanently in Karachi, so make sure you make a reservation before visiting. The absolute must-tries are the pâté, the chocolate mousse, and the delicious lobster.
restaurant:cafe-flo.location: Chartered Accountants Ave, near Teen Talwar (Three Swords) roundabout, Clifton.

paragraphs: This is one place that holds regular artistic and linguistic performances, exhibitions, and gatherings. There are sure to be a few art exhibitions, Mushaairaas, or other linguistic activities going on at any time of the year. Entrance into the Arts Council is generally free, although some events may require an entrance fee or a special invite.

This day is celebrated to mark the birth date of the Holy Prophet of Islam. It has become very popular in Karachi in recent years, and the preparations for the events themselves are a sight to behold. The major roads of the city are lit up with green lights right from the very beginning of the month. The most ornamentation is at the Numaish Roundabout, where large tents with heart-throbbing sound systems are set up on both sides of the wide road, playing Na’ats (songs in praise of the Prophet) all day long. On the 12th of Rabi-ul-Awwal there are processions along the M.A.Jinnah Road, the Golimaar-Lasbela-Gurumandar Road, and other prominent thoroughfares of the city. Thousands of people attend these processions, chanting religious anthems and slogans, and carrying large flags on high poles balanced by ropes held taut by energetic young men from all sides. There are large religious gatherings arranged at public parks throughout Karachi, but they are always a risky business. After the bomb blast at such a gathering at Nishtar Park, Saddar, just a few years back, these gatherings have raised questions about the security measures undertaken by the city government. It is therefore not advisable to attend such gatherings but to enjoy watching the sights from the safety of your balcony, or the television.

This event corresponds to the great sacrifice made by the Prophet Abraham to God. This is the most happening event of the Muslim year. It is marked with the sacrifice of livestock such as goats, cows, and camels. The meat obtained from the animal is divided into 3 parts: one is for personal consumption, one part is distributed amongst relatives and friends and the third is distributed among the poor. The events leading up to the big day are no less than a series of festivals. From the small grass and feed shops at every street corner to the large, sprawling cattle markets being set up on the outskirts of the city, the event is all about buying, feeding, and walking the animals that are to be slaughtered on the big day. As the day approaches, the amount of animals on the streets increases dramatically, and almost every street in the city can be seen teaming with cattle as though they were being bred in the very homes. The real fun is the night-time guarding of the animals. This has become an increasing concern after several events of cattle being stolen from the baraas they were kept in. Groups of young men set up small tents near large cattle baraas, with supplies for the night, including cups of tea, cigarettes, decks of cards, and of course motorbikes, to roam the city and keep watch. The activities on the actual day of Eid (10th Zil-Hajj) are limited to running after butchers to help get the animal slaughtered and dividing the meat according to the above-mentioned purposes. This continues for another two days, as the number of butchers in the city is too few to cope with the demand of the festival, and also because some people, according to various religious beliefs, prefer slaughtering their animals on the 2nd or 3rd day of Eid (11th or 12th Zil-Hajj). All camels are usually slaughtered on the third day and the whole neighborhood as well as people from nearby areas gather around to see the camel being sacrificed, as this is a sight they do not get to see very often.

This is the most important religious festival in Karachi, and all throughout the Muslim world. After a month of fasting Muslims all around the world celebrate Eid. In Karachi Eid is marked with the special Eid prayers being offered in large open grounds throughout the city at times that are convenient to almost everyone, starting from 7:15 in the morning to around 10:30 am. This means that if you miss the Eid prayer at one location you are almost certain to find another one being offered a little later somewhere close by. The prayer grounds are filled with vendors selling balloons, toys, confectionaries, and other such stuff, to attract the small children who tag along with their fathers to attend the mass congregation. Also to be found are seemingly never-ending queues of beggars of every kind. Eid is usually a very family-oriented occasion. There are visits to relatives, neighbors, and friends, and this day is one of those few moments that you get to spend some quality time with people who deserve your love and attention. All major shopping centers and markets usually remain closed on the first day of Eid, and business activities resume after the 3rd day.

The most important festival of Pakistan, the National Day, stirs in every citizen’s heart a feeling of fierce patriotism and passion. It is marked with celebrations throughout the city. Typical sights include ornamentations on government buildings, functions in schools, patriotic concerts at popular youth hangouts, and silencer-less bikes speeding past you every five minutes, driven by some guy with a funky shirt, sunglasses, and a bandana made from the flag. The day begins with the national anthem being broadcast at 8:00 am live from the capital city of Islamabad. After the initial celebrations in the capital city, there is a special ceremony held at the Mazar-e-Quaid, usually presided over by the president of the country. Also present are prominent officials like the Prime Minister, provincial governor, and city mayor. There are unofficial food festivals held at the food streets in Karachi, and many popular food outlets usually come up with some ‘Azadi’ package, which offers discounts or new deals to customers. The city is decorated with buntings and flags, and once again the lights on the various government buildings throughout the city are a sight to cherish at night. A night-time visit to the KMC Building (M.A.Jinnah Road) and the Sindh Secratariat Buildings (Secratariat Road, off Frere Road) is recommended.

Atrium Mall is most famously known for its cinema, as it is Pakistan’s very first 3D cinema with showings of some of the recent movie releases. It has very limited retail outlets but it does have a food court that suits all taste buds. This mall is located in Saddar, the busiest part of Karachi.
Timings: 11am – midnight

This relatively new, hotel-turned office building is located next to one of the most renowned roundabouts in Karachi – the ‘dou talwar’ or ‘two swords’. This 393-foot high building is the country’s tallest building in all of Pakistan, with the first four levels dedicated to retail outlets and the fifth a food court, accompanied by one of the largest cinemas in Karachi (Cinepax). The basement level consists of a popular supermarket known as ‘Farid’s’ that consists of all your everyday grocery needs.
Timings: 10am – midnight daily

Opened in 2011, Dolmen Mall is the first mall in Karachi that brought international fashion brand outlets such as Debenhams, Mango, Next, Pedro, Charles and Keith, River Island, Monsoon, Crocs and many more to Karachi. It also consists of a variety of international food chains such as Noodle House and Caribou Coffee, and for the first time in the country, Fat Burger, Solen Istanbul, Cinnabon and Papa Roti.

Dolmen Mall also has a large internationally acknowledged hypermarket, Hyperstar, which offers shoppers a variety of products – from meats and frozen food to electronics and clothing. It also has a popular kid’s arcade known as ‘Sinbad’ for families to enjoy.

Dolmen Mall in Karachi is comfortably located at Seaview, Clifton and is centrally air conditioned with a two-story underground parking lot. It has an entrance security checkpoint and disallows armed security guards and on certain days only allows families to enter – single men are deprived entry.
Timings: 10am to midnight daily

Boulton Market is a large – no, very large – wholesale market, one of the oldest markets in Karachi. It has a huge variety of items available in bulk quantities at very low prices. Some items of interest include toys, old books, caps and hats, shoes, and electronic items (non-warranted, obviously). It has now become the unofficial “trade route” that links Karachi to Chinese markets, and Chinese goods ranging from purely ornamental translucent “gel balls” to highly functional multimedia systems are all available here at very low prices. The popular “Everything China” is a phrase that most probably evolved from this market.

CK, Gucci, Levi’s, BVLGARI, and any other brand that you know of – no, this is not Beverly Hills, this is our very own Zainab Market – the hub of “pirated” branded clothes, home to jeans, shirts, trousers, formal attire, caps, sunglasses, belts, and all other “fashion” items. Hit Zainab Market for a complete makeover, and you could be the next rags-to-riches Hollywood star (in just under a few thousand rupees)!

Another one of the “obscure” markets of Karachi. While passing by it looks like any other flea market but once you get off your ride and enter the small sidestreets, a wealth of variety awaits you. A large collection of items of daily use at pretty cheap prices, including crockery, spices, grocery, and, as usual, a huge variety of clothes, ranging from casual to extremely exquisite, formal and bridal wear.

Rumored to be the oldest women’s market in Karachi, Meena Bazaar has lived up to its name. It is extremely cheap, and has everything for women. The expense of the market is rather difficult to judge from the road that approaches it, but once you leave the main road and enter the narrow alleyways that meander through the market you will simply be awed by the creative utilization of the limited space by the shopkeepers. Not really recommended if you suffer from asthma or sinus problems.

By its looks, a mall that might have been designed to rival Park Towers of Clifton, but has not been quite successful in doing so. Its fascinating purple facade with dark purple tinted glass fenestration has somehow taken the effect of the dull high-rise apartments nearby, and the mall has lost its original lucrative look. Still worth a visit as a shopping spot. Centrally air conditioned, but parking may pose a problem.
Expansive market, good bargains. Everything from clothes to shoes to accessories, and a lot of food vendors whose delicacies it is definitely exciting to try.

A downtown, easily accessible shopping district which is a sure treat for all the family. It has the latest in fashion, food, and music/entertainment. With large malls and small shops alike, Tariq Road is the typical “upper middle class” shopping experience. Bargaining is highly advised, especially out on the street shops! Dolmen Mall is a must-visit anytime you hit Tariq Road. Centrally air-conditioned, it has a few designer outlets and a lot of local shops offering clothes to toys to health products to music/film/entertainment stuff. The spacious food court on the top floor has some popular food outlets including Indulge and KFC, as well as some local diners.
Another one of the few lavish, centrally air-coditioned malls in Karachi. Lots of designer gear, and some local shops offering bargains. Has an incredible and inexpensive food court.

A very expensive shopping neighborhood, but worth a visit if you’re not short on cash. Located in the heart of the DHA/Clifton area, it offers many international designer shops, and a lot of (pretty expensive) eating places including Arizona Grill, Costa Coffee, and Copper Kettle.

Large, ideally located, and centrally air-conditioned, this is one of the ultimate shopping destinations for Karachi’s elite! Has designer shops, including clothing and accessories, music and book stores, and a delightful food court. Open till late, 7 days a week.
Sundays are ‘family days’ – groups with females and/or kids only are allowed to enter.

Hotel Sarawan has 90 air-conditioned rooms, standard and double, with cable TV, hot and cold water, mini fridge and intercom. Additional facilities include laundry service, doctor on call, car parking and rent a car. The hotel also has a meeting room and banquet hall as well as a restaurant. Credit cards are accepted.

Conveniently located on the Main Shahrah-e-Faisal, the most prestigeous and secured road coming straight from the International Airport and very near the famous shopping and commercial areas. Only 20 minutes drive straight from Quaid-e-Azam International Airport and 5 minutes drive to Tariq Road, the most popular shopping street of Karachi.

Rooms: 80 centrally air conditioned guest rooms, completely renovated and totally refurbished with latest style comfort and modern facilities, having internet access with choice of single, twin and double rooms. Some facilities include thermostatically controlled room temperature, sound proof windows, TV (up to 100 channels) and mini fridge.

Dining: Marvi One has a capacity of 50 persons and offers a la cart lunch and dinner. Special Chinese food, Pakistani delicacies, Italian pizzas, chef’s recommendations, deserts and beverages are yours to savor. Marvi Two offers a buffet breakfast and has a capacity of 60 – 70 persons. Arrangments for small parties for lunch and dinner are also undertaken. The 24 hour coffee shop serves sandwiches & burgers, special deserts, tea, coffee, soft drinks, fresh seasonal juices and shakes.
Meeting & Banquet: The hotel has banquet facilities for up to 200 persons. Conference and meeting rooms make this the ideal choice for business travelers.
hotel:embassy-inn.location: 100-B, S.M.C.H.S. Nursery, Main Shahrah-e-Faisal, KARACHI – 74400. PAKISTAN.

paragraphs: Ideally located in the heart of Karachi, Hotel Mehran provides its guests an uncompromising level of services – personal yet efficient and swift. This elegant hotel offers a very relaxed atmosphere and charm. Ideally located, it is just twenty minutes drive from the airport and close to the city’s central business and industrial districts and conveniently close to the finest shopping area.

Rooms: 182 guest rooms including executive floors with executive rooms with top class amenities including cable TV, in-room movies, coffee maker, free morning newspaper delivered to the room, mini bar, etc.
Dining: Kanwal Restaurant – 24 Hour Coffee Shop, Hotel Lobby Level. Serves Breakfast, Lunch and Dinner. Cuisine: Coffeehouse, Pakistani, International, Local, Vegetarian.

Meeting/Banquet: The hotel provides complete banquet and conference facilities and support equipment for top level executives. There are conference rooms on almost every floor of the hotel. Additionally, on level one there is an open banquet space with a capacity of 500 people to host large functions like weddings, etc.

This hotel, unlike the rest of the notable hotels in Karachi, is not located around the Saddar district or adjoining areas. It is set in a very different environment, away from the bustling city, near the coast of the Arabian Sea. Although not very large, it offers everything a traveler could wish for.

Rooms: It has a total of 82 rooms, divided into the regular Deluxe Rooms, the special Executive Rooms, and the luxurious Beach Suites. All these rooms offer magnificent views of the sea. All offer facilities like 24-hour direct dial telephone, cable television, and in-room electronic safe. A mini-bar is available on request.

Dining: 007 Restaurant: Open daily. Traditional and international cuisine. Buffet breakfast and lunch. Highly popular with the business class. BarBQ Seafront: Open air, floating BBQ restaurant. Continental food. Buffet dinners. Live music. Ideal for families. Casbah: Open daily. Open-air restaurant. A la carte dinners. Seafood and barbecue. American Terrace: Open daily. Open air restaurant. Finger foods, snacks, beverages.

Meetings & Events: The Beach Luxury Hotel offers a very different setting for all your meetings/events. Apart from the normal meeting/conference halls that all hotels offer, this place has lush lawns and gardens that can be utilized for such events. The hotel also has a team of experienced event managers that you can hire.

Unlike the other major hotels of Karachi that are located in the busy Saddar district and adjoining areas, Regent Plaza is situated conveniently on main Shahrah-e-Faisal, only a 15 minutes drive from the airport. The majestic facade, representing an elegant (and extremely large) cruise ship, makes the hotel difficult to miss, as it stands out amongst the surrounding buildings. The hotel contains 440 well-maintained rooms that meet the needs of all kinds of guests. Quite recently, many of the rooms have been refurbished substantially to improve the hotel’s quality standards.

Rooms: Standard rooms are single and/or twin bedrooms – well-decorated, comfortable, and economical, and contain an electronically operated digital safe to keep valuables, with daily newspapers delivered every morning. Executive Rooms (161) are for the business traveler, equipped with 24-hour internet and phone connections and a digital safe. Executive Suites (16) offer luxury and elegance, with additional amenities such as a parlor.

Health Facilities: The health center includes a fully equipped gymnasium, with a wide range of facilities for ladies and gents. This includes a Jacuzzi, Sauna and Steam Bath, and a large swimming pool. Also included are two air-conditioned squash courts. Professionally trained instructors are available to help improve your game.

Dining: Jharoka Coffee Shop is open all day with a traditional Pakistani as well as Continental menu, fast food, ice cream and shakes. Almas Restaurant serves formal lunches and dinners next to the pool. The Splash, by the pool, serves snacks and high tea. La Cream offers mouthwatering desserts and confectionaries, including sugar free delicacies.

Meetings & Events: Kohinoor Hall is the largest pillarless hall in Pakistan, over 15000 sq ft, catering to around 1200 people. The Crown Ball Room is another pillarless hall (approx 6000 sq ft) for above 500 people, ideal for smaller conferences, lectures, and get-togethers.
hotel:regent-plaza-hotel.location: Regent Plaza Hotel & Convention Centre, Main Shahrah-e-Faisal, Karachi.

paragraphs: 5 star landmark hotel located in the heart of Karachi. With an outstanding choice of accommodations, recreation, conference & banqueting facilities and a great choice of food & beverage venues, the Avari Towers is the perfect choice during your stay in Karachi. Shopping centres, business districts, Karachi Port and crisp beaches are all easily accessible from the hotel.

Rooms: There are 236 bedrooms offering minibar, TV with satellite channels, DVD/VCR, telephone with voice mail and data port, in room digital safe deposit box and tea/coffee making facilities. There is also a daily maid service. Free newspapers are delivered daily. All rooms offer wireless internet access complimentary while E-Rooms include Laser Printers. Lady Avari are rooms exclusively for lady travelers offering additional facilities and amenities catering to their needs.
Health Facilities: On site facilities include bicycling, game room, health club, health spa/massage, pool (children’s pool & outdoor pool) and outdoor tennis.
Dining: The hotel offers a selection of restaurants including Chinese, Japanese, Pakistani and Continental.
Meetings: This hotel has all modern facilities required to cater to your business/banqueting needs.

The Marriott is located right across the magnificent Frere Hall in the very heart of Karachi. It is connected by major road networks, offering convenient access to all parts of the city by roads that are relatively less crammed with traffic. Offers on-site parking for free.

Rooms: The Guest Rooms at Marriott provide every comfort a foreigner could wish for. From the very basic, like air-conditioning and cable television, to unique, like mini-bar and climate control features, to the very exclusive, like the business-class speaker phone, dual-line phone, and voice mail. 24 hour room service is provided by a very professional, and devoted, team. The rooms are also connected to a high-speed internet system (both wired and wireless) for business/corporate guests.

Dining: Dumpukht Restaurant offers an oriental ambience to relive exquisite Mughal mealtimes (lunch and dinner). Nadia Cafe Restaurant is a small coffee house offering Pakistani as well as International treats (breakfast, lunch, dinner). Suzie Wong serves authentic Chinese dishes (lunch and dinner).

Meetings & Events: The Marriott Hotel has a multipurpose Business Center, with comprehensive modern-day facilities, including state-of-the-art multimedia resources such as computers, projectors, internet facilities, and audiovisual systems. There are nine individual meeting rooms/spaces. The largest, the Crystal Ballroom, has a floor space of above 5400 sq m, and can accommodate a maximum of 800 people. The hotel also offers special services such as wedding planning and event management.

This is another of the few five star hotels in Karachi and is located across the road from Sheraton Hotel. Its location in the very center of the busy commercial district of Karachi makes it a perfect stop for tourists. No doubt it is expensive but definitely worth it. It is also one of the most secure hotels given the current unstable political situation of the country.

Rooms: All rooms are equipped with modern amenities like air-conditioning, local telephone, and room service. Deluxe Rooms are economical for a small family or a single traveler, with mini-bar, wireless internet, plasma screen TV with international cable connection, 24 hour in-room dining, phone and fax, and laundry and butler on demand. Executive Rooms include all the amenities of the Deluxe Room, plus 24 hour personalized butler service and free local telephone calls. Luxury Suites are larger, with a spacious living room, dining area, guest bathrooms, and large bedrooms with views of the cityscape.

Health Facilities: The health facilities include a world-class gym (with separate timings for ladies and gents), swimming pool, table tennis and a Spa and Health Club. The Fitness Center offers specialized equipment as well as weight-training and bodybuilding equipment. The Sauna, Steam Bath, and Jacuzzi, and the clear and cool swimming pool offer a relaxing experience.

Dining: The Pearl Continental has seven unique and elegant restaurants, ranging from coffee shops and light finger foods to whole hearted buffets. You can also choose to have food delivered to your own room any time of the day.

Meetings & Events: The hotel offers highly equipped meeting rooms and spacious banquet halls, including a multimedia system, several kinds of projectors, laser pointing facilities, and videoconferencing with an impressive sound system. The Grand Ballrooms are an ideal choice for exhibitions, get-togethers, and traditional events such as wedding ceremonies.

On January 1 2014 Karachi’s prestigious Sheraton Hotel changed management and was renamed Movenpick Hotel Karachi. This is one of the few hotels in Pakistan that is internationally managed. It is a luxurious five-star hotel and is one of the most secure hotels in Karachi. It is frequented by many international and local businessmen.

Located about 15 kilometers from the airport, right in the heart of Karachi’s commercial hub with easy access to local shopping hotspots and tourist landmarks makes it the first preference for foreigners.

With 407 rooms, including one and two bedroom suites, the Movenpick Hotel is geared to meet the requirements of its upscale clientele. All Deluxe and Executive rooms come with free internet. Those staying in the Executive rooms have access to the Executive Lounge which is an ideal place for business travelers.
hotel:movenpick-hotel-karachi.location: Club Road, P.O. Box 3918, Karachi, Sind 75530, Pakistan.

masjid:memon-masjid.location: M. A. Jinnah Rd, Karachi, Pakistan

masjid:masjid-e-tooba-gol-masjid.location: Old Korangi Road, Phase-II, Karachi 74000, Sindh, Pakistan

paragraphs: Legacy Irhal treats Karachi with children as a mix of simple neighborhood discovery and purpose-built family stops. The original guide points families toward local parks, street cricket, amusement and recreation parks, beach outings, Safari Park, Hill Park, Go Aish Adventure Park, and Karachi Zoo/Gandhi Garden.

Use this roundup as a planning hub rather than a single venue. Combine one educational or animal-focused stop with one outdoor break and one easy food or mall stop, then verify opening hours, rides, safety conditions, ticketing, and weather close to the day of travel.

Located near the NIPA roundabout in the Gulshan area, this is a large area dedicated to protecting some species of wildlife. Attractions for children include a mini-train service giving a tour of the whole park, a Safari bus that takes them inside the animal shelters, and a play area.

Located in the Garden area near Saddar, this is the primary zoo in Karachi. Tickets are conveniently cheap, which means that all kinds of people can make their way in. But special days are allocated for families, so you should feel safe if you visit on the family days. A play area is included for children, as well as several large animal statues which never fail to attract children to ‘get on’ them.

The beach, commonly called Clifton, is a long stretch of shore. It extends from southern parts of Karachi to the northwestern Karachi coasts, where it merges with the eastern coasts of Baluchistan. The common areas of the beach ideal for recreation include: The Clifton area, also called Seaview; The Sandspit area (a long drive from the main Clifton beach, much quieter and calmer, ideal picnic spot if you can rent a hut there); Hawkesbay area (clean sandy shores and clear and cool waters; also is a natural breeding ground for the endangered Green Turtle); Paradise Point (famous for high tides and dangerous rocks which have been severely eroded by water and wind to form natural caves and arches); Gadani (just outside Karachi, inside Baluchistan; famous for its rocks and clear waters).

If you happen to visit Karachi during the peak summer months, be sure you do not miss out on the beach. The Clifton/Seaview area is the most popular hangout in the summers, and it is very crowded. Food stalls and green tea vendors can be seen everywhere. Horse and camel rides are ready to be taken, at just around Rs 30-50. If you want to avoid crowds, it is better to head for the Sandspit or the Hawkesbay area, which are not very far. And if you want to dive into total solitary serenity, make your way to Gadani or Paradise Point. But make sure you travel with reliable people, as these areas can be dangerous, especially for foreigners.
